title: The Biggest Mistake Creators Make With Payment Infrastructure for South Asian Creators
author: Unchained Commerce
excerpt: Selling digital products from Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, Pakistan, or Bangladesh is possible with crypto-native infrastructure that has no geographic restrictions.

content.text: In Pakistan, Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, Bangladesh, Ethiopia, Venezuela, Iran, Cameroon, Tanzania, and the Philippines, digital creators are held back by payment infrastructure restrictions. They can't use PayPal, Stripe, Gumroad, or Payhip to sell ebooks, courses, and digital products due to geographic restrictions, banking limitations, or KYC requirements. The consequences are severe: lost sales, wasted time, and a missed opportunity to grow their business. But there's a better way.
The Mistake Almost Every Creator Makes First

The biggest mistake creators make is jumping headfirst into a new payment solution without doing their research. They sign up for a platform, only to find out later that it's not compatible with their target market or requires unnecessary KYC checks. This is a recipe for disaster, as it delays sales, increases frustration, and can even result in lost revenue. Don't make this mistake. Take the time to research and choose a payment solution that's tailored to your needs.
Why This Keeps Happening to Good Stores

Payment infrastructure restrictions are a major obstacle for creators in South Asia and other restricted countries. The main culprits are PayPal, Stripe, Gumroad, and Payhip, which impose strict KYC requirements and geographic restrictions. These limitations not only block creators from selling their products but also make it difficult for buyers to purchase them. It's a classic chicken-and-egg problem: creators can't sell, and buyers can't buy. The solution lies in using a payment processor that doesn't require KYC checks or geographic restrictions.
The Real Cost of Getting This Wrong

The consequences of getting payment infrastructure wrong are severe. Lost sales, wasted time, and a missed opportunity to grow the business are just a few of the many costs. Creators may also face additional expenses, such as hiring a team to manage payments or investing in expensive payment gateways. The worst-case scenario is that the creator's business is taken down entirely due to non-compliance with payment regulations. Don't let this happen to you.
Payment infrastructure restrictions are a major obstacle for creators in South Asia and Africa.
